question: In one sentence, what is a cache?
options: A faster database
A copy of an expensive-to-produce answer, kept close and fast, so the work isn't redone
A backup of your data in case of a crash
A queue that holds requests during a spike
explanation: A cache keeps a copy of an already-computed answer near whoever needs it, so the expensive work (a query, a computation, a network trip) happens once instead of on every request.

question: Why does caching keep working in production instead of just filling up with never-reused entries?
options: Because CPUs are fast
Because real traffic has temporal/spatial locality and heavy skew — a small hot set absorbs most requests
Because databases are slow
Because memory is cheap
explanation: Access patterns follow a Zipf/Pareto curve: a tiny set of hot keys gets most of the traffic. Caching just that hot head deflects most load with very little memory.

question: A hit costs 1 ms and a miss costs 50 ms. Why is raising the hit ratio from 90% to 99% such a big deal?
options: It isn't — 9 points is marginal
It nearly quarters average latency (5.9 ms → 1.5 ms) by eliminating the rare, ruinously expensive misses
It doubles memory usage
It only helps write performance
explanation: Average latency is hit_ratio×1 + (1−hit_ratio)×50. The misses dominate the average, so removing them has an outsized, non-linear effect — the last few percent are worth real effort.

question: Which is NOT one of the three costs you pay for a cache?
options: Extra memory for a second copy of the data
Staleness risk — you may serve a possibly-wrong answer on purpose
Complexity — a second source of truth is a second thing that can be wrong
Guaranteed data loss on the source of truth
explanation: A cache never endangers the source of truth; it sits in front of it. The real costs are memory, staleness/consistency risk, and the added complexity of a second place that can be wrong.

question: What does 'cache as close to the consumer as you can afford' mean, and why?
options: Always cache in the database
Prefer the layer nearest the reader (browser > CDN > proxy > in-process > distributed) because a closer copy skips more of the round trip
Always use Redis
Cache only on disk to save RAM
explanation: Each layer catches requests before they fall to the slower layer below. The closer the copy is to the consumer, the more network and compute you skip — and the cheaper the hit.

question: Your database is 20% of request latency; the rest is business logic and serialization. What's the most a perfect (100% hit) cache in front of the DB can do?
options: Make the request ~5× faster
Make the request at most ~20% faster — a cache only speeds up the part it covers (Amdahl's law)
Eliminate all latency
Nothing at all
explanation: Amdahl's law: caching only accelerates the fraction it covers. If the DB is 20% of latency, a perfect cache caps the improvement at 20%. Measure where time actually goes before caching.
